sentences.text: Well, I knew that a war was coming.
And I remember we were coming back from an outing in Buda, to our apartment in Pest, and we were walking and the window was open in the house.
And the radio announced that the Second World War had begun.
And although I was only 11, I was-- I had been a-- student is too strong a word.
As a boy, I read a great deal about the First World War.
The First World War was a very traumatic event for Hungary because Hungary lost huge portions of its territory and its population.
And I had several friends whose fathers were in the war, and I-- I-- I read a great deal about the war, heard a great deal about the war.
And I knew that it-- it is horrible and with the new weapons, it will be more horrible.

I followed, as a boy, the Spanish Civil War, so I-- I-- I knew about the-- the new power of the-- of the-- of the military airplane and what havoc it can create and the increase in firepower and all that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I-- I did not have a feeling of personal fear.
I mean, I didn't, at that time, view myself as being in particular danger.
I realized that Hungary will be an ally of Hitler and Mussolini.

As a matter of fact, you know, that's a rather interesting point because one of the great joys for me of being in the United States-- and I am patriotic beyond anything you can imagine-- is that when I came to this country, 1947, and then subsequently I became a citizen.
For the first time in my life, I was on the side where I wanted to be.
Because those early years, I mean, particularly during the war, I was, of course, hoping and praying and rooting and working to have the other side win.
And this became particularly poignant when I was in a forced labor camp north of Budapest, where the British and American Air Forces were bombing us all the time.
And I had very mixed feelings because I wanted the-- the bombing raids to be very successful in that the railroad be destroyed, on which I was working.
But still, you were hoping that you might survive.
I-- I really was too busy with my day-to-day life to have, you know, long-term fears or concerns.
I-- I knew the times-- that the times will be difficult and they'd be particularly difficult for Jews.
Yes, I was.
Oh, I had personal experiences in-- with that and-- and let me tell you what they are.
It's one of my most vivid memories.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In what used to be North East Hungary, there is a fairly major town, the Czechoslovak name for it is Kosice.
The Hungarian name is Kassa.
My younger-- my young uncle was a high school teacher and his high school was one of the high schools that went up to Kassa, Kosice, when the Hungarians took it over.
And I went with them.
So I was part of this unbelievable, you know, German, Hungarian fascist invasion of democratic Czechoslovakia.
I was an unwitting, unwilling participant, waving the Hungarian flag.
I remember when Mussolini's son-in-law, Count Ciano, the Italian foreign minister, came to Budapest.
And my school was one of the schools that lined the main street, [INAUDIBLE], which is a beautiful boulevard.
And I had a Hungarian flag in one hand and an Italian flag in another-- in the other hand.
And I was chanting, Ciano, Ciano, Ciano, Ciano.
So I-- I was more than aware.
I was a participant.
As all of us kids were.
Not personally.
Not personally.
We-- we were aware.
We followed events.
The BBC was sort of our daily avenue to truth.
And I listened to it.
I can still see this large radio set we had, and it had a sort of a-- a colored seeing eye.
And when it was closed, then you got perfect reception.
It was very difficult, as we had to juggle the tuning.
And I became very good at it.

And we always, depending on how far along the-- the war and the German and the Nazi suppression was, we closed the curtains and-- and lowered the-- the shades and-- and sort of huddled around this set and listened.
So the answer is yes.
We followed things and we followed them on maps.
And we were-- we were hoping and praying for the British and the French and then the Russians and then the Americans to be victorious.

Much later, I must tell you, as a member of Congress, when I took congressional delegations to the Soviet Union, I always got a very stunned and startled reaction from my Soviet hosts when I said that I'm the only member of Congress who owes his life to the Soviet Army.

And-- and to make it even more specific, both my friend Sharansky, who is now Minister of Trade and Industry in Israel, and I believe that his father was part of the military unit that liberated me in Budapest in January '45.
Yeah.
Yeah, we had a long talk, Sharansky and I, about his father and his father's whereabouts during this period.
And-- and we believe he was part of the unit that liberated me.
Yes.
Yes.
I have to be very careful not to give my current interpretation of what I thought at the time.
As I recall, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we became increasingly more and more aware of the-- of the nightmare quality of this man, his hatred, his venom, his viciousness.

But through some mysterious disconnect, which to this moment, I cannot explain or understand, it seemed implausible to my highly civilized, cultured, democratically inclined, humanitarian family as to what the final outcome could be.
It-- it-- it did not seem plausible.

And-- and I have a great deal of difficulty giving you a rational or logical explanation because-- because the people I was listening to and I was-- I obviously was just a boy so I didn't participate.
I listened more than I spoke, obviously.
These were very intelligent people.
But obviously blind.
Not personally.
No, I don't recall any personally, no.
Well, it really came in-- in three segments.
The first one was the calling up of my uncle and cousins for labor-- compulsory labor service on the Russian front.
And all of our attention, of course, was devoted to them-- how to get a package to them, can we get mail from them, and so on.
And this was highly personal because this was a very tight knit family.
And the four young men in the family, my uncle and the three boys, who were-- who were anywhere from-- anywhere from six to 15 years older than I was.
That's why I wasn't part of this group.
Our whole life focused on them I mean, that-- that was very personal.
And it became increasingly more and more bleak as to whether they will ever come back.
Of course, while we were hoping long after the war that somebody will turn up, nobody ever did.
So that was the first personal impact.
Well, the government-- the government was very self righteous about it.
I mean, Hungary was an ally of Germany.
The Hungarian troops fought alongside the German troops.
Jews were not trustworthy to fight.
They could not bear arms, but they had to carry the burden of doing the dirty work for the Hungarian army.
I mean, there's no secret about it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember songs that Hungarian soldiers coming back from the Russian front were singing.
And I remember one song in particular, which said that I will be-- I will be a barber, [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN].
And his job will be to slit every Jew's throat.
I'll never forget that song.
I heard it many times.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The second personal impact came on March 19, 1944 when Hitler's troops occupied Hungary.
And, of course, this represented the change in the-- in the fate of Hungarian Jews.
And I won't repeat the historic facts because we all know them.
The countryside Jews were all herded into railroad cars and shipped to Auschwitz and other places.
And that's when Wallenberg came and helped make the city, Jewish population in Budapest survived.
That is when I was put into a forced labor camp, so that became very personal.
I-- I-- I recall having a document distributed in the house where we were living that all Jews between certain ages-- and I fell into that category-- had to report.
And it was a quasi-military operation, and I was-- I was inducted into a forced labor battalion.
I was taken up north of Budapest.
The Danube makes a 90 degree turn.
We call it the Danube bend.
There are important railroad bridges there and so on.
And I was there, working on the railroad--
--repairing the damage.
Well, I was-- I was in the sixth of the eighth-- eight grades of the high school.
But obviously, that all came to a screeching halt.
I mean, I was pushed into this forced labor camp along with everybody else.
So that was the second phase.
And while quite a few of my colleagues were killed as a result of-- of aerial bombardment, I was-- I also worked on a-- on a-- on a heavy industrial island near Budapest called Csepel.
I worked there as a forced labor battalion person.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Between the March 19, 1944 and October 15, 1994, Hungary basically still had a non-bloodthirsty leader in the person of Admiral Horthy, who was anti-Semitic but not in a bloodthirsty fashion.
On October 15, 1944, when the Germans got wind that he is attempting to make a separate peace with the Allies, they arrested him.
They arrested his son.
They-- they-- they organized a-- a coup.
And the most vicious Hungarian fascist, the head of the Arrow Cross party, Szalasi took over.
And at that point, Jews became even more endangered.
So my own personal involvement, and I don't attach much importance to-- to what happened to me personally.
Quite frankly, I didn't expect to survive after awhile.
I mean, many of the things that I did, while they were useful to people, they were not courageous because I was convinced, as 1944 dragged on, that my chances of survival were nil.
So let's go back to the first time when you entered the labor force.
Where did-- where did they take you?
They-- they took--
Well, they-- they-- they took us to a small town, north of Budapest, called Szob, S-Z-O-B.
We were put into work battalions, and we had to work the railroad.
The railroad was being bombed, and we had to repair.
We lived in-- under very primitive circumstances.
We got very little and low quality food.
Occasionally we were beaten.
We were treated viciously.
By the Hungarian Arrow Cross guards who ran this labor battalion camp.
But even that period did not give me an inkling that there is-- there is a gas chamber at the end of this-- of this line.
I mean, this this was-- this was still profoundly different from what I, in retrospect, now view as mass extermination.
I mean, my friend, Elie Wiesel, had a very different set of experiences from me.
Mine was not hell, it was purgatory.
I had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sporadic and minimal contact.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They sent me, occasionally, a note, bribing people to get it to me.
I-- I escaped once.
Was caught.
Was beaten to a pulp.
At night.
You know, this is not a long distance.
This town, maybe 30 kilometers north of Budapest, so it's walking distance.
I was caught.
I was put back.
And-- and my friend and I were beaten together.
It was a-- he-- he as a physician now, says he doesn't understand how we survived it.
It was so-- such a brutal beating.
Yes?
Well, I considered the consequences, but I also saw that there was no hope for me staying there.
It was clear for-- I was-- I became convinced, as the spring of 1944 unfolded, that I will not live through the year.
I-- I don't know whether the word fatalism fully expresses the feeling I had.
But I sort of looked at myself, almost from the outside.
One of my favorite British authors is Chaucer.
And one of the things I like about Chaucer is that he looks at life as if he would be sitting on a fence, watching this parade go by.
And he himself is part of this parade, but at the same time, he's watching the parade.
And I had a very similar feeling.
I mean, I-- I-- I knew I was part of this labor battalion and we were as low as dirt.
And if one of us died or one of us was killed or one of us was beaten, nobody paid any attention.
And I knew that will be my fate.
At the same time, I was watching this whole thing.
And I suspect that helped me.
And it has helped me since.
I mean, what the Holocaust has done, it seems to me, to various people, is very different things.
I mean, some people have been crushed by the Holocaust.
I mean survivors been crushed by the Holocaust-- either physically or mentally or psychologically or-- or all three.
Some-- and I'm certainly not one of them-- have turned to religion.
If anything, I've turned away from religion because I cannot reconcile faith and the Holocaust.

Some of us-- and I put myself into this category, and I don't know whether this is 1%, 10%, I don't think it's much higher than that-- have been made enormously resilient by-- by this experience, which speaking for myself, I still don't fully understand, although I read 100 books about it.
I cannot make sense of it.
But what I do is-- and this is, of course, why, thanks to my wife, much of my work has focused on human rights.
I mean, I am-- I shouldn't say I am.
My wife and because of my wife, my office has become the voice of human rights in the Congress of the United States-- human rights for Tibetans and-- and persecuted people all over.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I view this as a moral obligation.

I realize that I'm making a very marginal difference, a very tiny difference, but I can't live my-- with myself if I don't make the maximum difference I've been privileged to be able to do as a member of Congress.
So I am very grateful that I survived, much less in a personal sense, but because I am sort of part of the forces of good in this world.
You know, many Holocaust survivors-- I mean a person like Elie Wiesel is the chronicler of the Holocaust.
He's the conscience of the Holocaust.
And I've enormous admiration for Elie and for what-- what he has done, his enormous talents.
Some have been financially successful and have been philanthropists for good causes.
And I've great respect for them.
I have no money.
My-- my-- my work is in the Congress.
And through my work in the Congress, it really focuses on human rights.
So what the-- what the Holocaust has done to me, it made me a human rights activist.
And it took me some time to-- to physically recover and then I was sent back to work.
And shortly thereafter, I successfully escaped.
Made my way to a-- a Wallenberg protected house, number 25 Saint Stephen Park, very near the bank of the Danube where my aunt and uncle had an apartment.
But of course by this time, it was not their apartment-- 25 people, 30 people lived in a place that plus for two people or three people.
And I became part of this group.
I did not-- it's very difficult for me to distinguish between what I know now and what I knew then.
So I want to be very cautious.

The first thing I want to say is that although I worked with Wallenberg, and I was one of the young men who helped his operation, I was a young kid, one of hundreds, so I was in no sense a collaborator of his.
I was-- I was a tiny, tiny cog in a vast apparatus.
And my principal virtue, I think was two-fold.
One, that I looked very Aryan.
I-- I looked as-- I looked like a Hitlerjugend.
I was blue eyed, blond, tall with very Nordic features, so I could be used as a messenger.
I was sent out to collect-- my-- my most intense memories relate to collecting medications for people, various medications.
The one person I remember most was a middle-aged man who had the regular asthma attacks.
I'd never seen an asthma attack before this gentleman came into my life or I came into his life.
And he had to lie down on the floor and was gasping for air.
And I was-- the-- the person who sort of organized this house, they gave me the task of getting medications for all the people who needed medications.
And the reason I could do that was because I-- I had a sort of a-- not a military uniform, sort of a-- and not a Boy Scout uniform.
They-- they called it the [? levente ?] uniform.
Levente ?] was a-- a Hungarian historic term.
It's sort of a cadet, a-- a military cadet uniform with a Hungarian motifs and very handsome cap.
And I move around the city.
And nobody bothered me, although I knew full well, because only Jews were circumcised, that if there was any suspicion and I had to drop my pants, that would be the end.
They-- they never asked me.
And I think that's why I survived, because I moved around the city throughout the worst period, both the Horthy period and the Szalasi period, to the very, very bitter end.
I did so, in retrospect, I'm convinced, not because of superior courage, but because of my conviction that I will not survive.
And I might as well be of some help, of some use.
I don't want to exaggerate the importance of the things I did.
I-- I-- I was carrying messages to Buda, to Pest, to-- to various people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I was basically collecting medicine, collecting food.
Taking messages to various people was a very important function.
I did not engage in any military activity.
I-- I-- but I was-- I suspect it's probably fair to say I was part of the anti-Nazi underground, as a 16-year-old boy, weakening their efforts and strengthening the resistance.
